speaker: interviewee
text: Let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me go back to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You asked me about life over there. It sounds like--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll ask you to finish about when you were found out, and then we're going to go back. When the Barniche's son followed you and you were found out, you continued to do his homework and papers for school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right on through liberation. It just went on, but there were so many things at that point that I was already doing. I mean, when I came home from school, naturally, I mean, we didn't go out to-- we weren't allowed to go out. Many times I snuck out, and this is one of those times when I told you it happened that I was baptized in the doorway. But in the Barniche's home, which I neglected, was-- I mean, life didn't go on 24 hours a day without any situations happening. In those days, there were many, many searches being done by the Gestapo, and they knew Jews were being hidden in, in Gentile's home. And in-- I had mentioned that the Barniche had an older son. And in those days, able-bodied men, even though they weren't Jewish, Belgians were taken to work gangs taken out to ditch trenches-- dig trenches and labor camps. And they went and they stayed there for a month or two. I mean, they weren't treated like the Jews were treated, but certainly they were made to work for the Germans. And there came a time where their oldest son escaped from one of those camps. And of all the places to choose to come, he came back home to his parents. And we knew that with that happening, the first place that the Germans will be looking for him would be in the house. And with us hidden there, that would have presented quite a problem. So I had mentioned that Emile Barniche ran-- was augmenting his income by being quite handy with his hands. And he had built a hiding place under a wooden stairway where there was a-- best way I can describe it, a false door that we could go into, I'd, I'd say, a space four feet by four feet, where my mother and I, whenever there was a search and there were many at that point, you know, banging on the door, searching for, for the son.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in the period of time we were there, we probably had about 20 different searches. Not necessarily just because the son had escaped, it also happened that they were searching for Jews at that time also. But we must have had about 20 searches. And after the third or fourth search, where we came so very close to being discovered under the stairway, because we heard a, a German-- one of the soldiers or Gestapo, I don't know who they were. I didn't see their uniform-- banging on the stairway with his rifle. And he could see-- hear the hollowness. And I learned German by that time, and I understood very, very well what he had said. Let's check underneath for any, any hiding places. And I also remember him coming quite close, coming down. And I said, well, that was it. And a German officer screaming and yelling said, that's it. We've had it.  Let's go on. And he took him away from there, and we knew then that this wasn't the best place for us to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Barniche, again-- we had an attic in in that house. And what he did, he built behind the wall of the attic, a-- he put up a false wall-- a wooden, for lack of a better word right now, let's call it paneling with a space where you could lay down on the floor about two feet wide and about six feet long.

speaker: interviewee
text: But only if one body laid on top of the other could you hide behind this thing. But the best way to hide it was not to protrude it, so he had to take whatever space was left behind this wall, and build this false wall. And that's was going to be our hiding place from then on. And I remember the searches that came after where we were made to go up and be behind that wall. And this comes my second nightmare story is that I was laying on the floor, face down, and my mother on top of me face down. And the only thing that I saw, sometimes for hours, because it wasn't just the searches that, that made us stay there, we wanted to make sure it was safe hours after. So we stayed there sometimes for hours on end just laying flat, my mother on top of me and me just looking at this tile, which had the Fleur de lis. And it just escaped me later on as to why I always saw this pattern and I woke up in screaming and nightmares and so on. And then to jump over, when I finally visited the Barniche way after the war, 25-30 years later, where the house was totally and completely untouched. The Barniche were still alive, and they took my wife and I, who had heard many stories about that but had never seen it, up to the attic. The man had never taken away the paneling, had never hid the hiding place that we were at. And I took one look on the floor, and the tile had the Fleur de lis. And it just came back to me as this is why I was seeing this in my nightmares. And I must say, from that point on I never had the nightmare again. So that was very therapeutic for me and very healing. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the life in the Barniche, slaving. Did your life change with the Barniches after their older son returned home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there oldest son returned home for just a short period of time and then escaped, and I never saw him again. But the searches continued after his escape and his return over there. He picked up some clothes, stayed one night, I believe, and that just went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the money? Did it eventually run out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [SIGHS] Yes, and at that time, by that time, I, I think I had grown up quite a bit. And I had seen quite a bit. And I had seen, oh, [SIGHS] killings on the streets, Jews being dragged out of their homes or being found out, shootings, bombings. I grew up, and I grew up very, very fast. And I felt that I was the man of the house at that point. And I was-- if any-- if we were going to be saved or if anything needed to be done, it was my duty to do that and protect my mother as much as I could. She, by that time, just was-- she slept, she prayed, she cried, and she sat in that chair for three and a half years never, never to go out, never to go up anywhere, never to see or know anything, just hoping that someday this all will be over. The only thing that she knew about and the only time she moved out of that chair were at those searches and when we went up to the attic to be hidden there. So-

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss the money running out with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I told her that, you know, I don't know how much longer this is going to take and how long this war's going to go on, but the money is running out. And we know very well that these people aren't going to do this if we don't have-- if they don't have something to gain from it. And by that time also, food became rationed. And it was very difficult for even Belgian families with-- I believe stamps at that time were given-- ration stamps. And the families were counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was enough ration stamps to feed the family that they claimed, but certainly not enough to feed us. And in all fairness to them, there wasn't enough to feed us. So we weren't fed. Every so often, a crumb.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, as a matter of fact, I developed all kinds of diseases because the food that they threw at us, just as an aside, I mean, in those days they divided the, the flour that they were baking with. It was a mixture of white and brown flour, and the white was baked for them, the brown was given to us. And with this brown flour, I'll never forget that once it was cooked-- if you want to call it that or baked-- you could make a bowl from it. And it was rubber. And this is what we ate. We ate anything that we could, and then I ate anything that I could steal. And this is where it all began, because just before it ended, and I could see maybe four or five coins left, which meant four or five months left. And the fact that we weren't eating anymore and concentration camp or not, we could fade away right there and die in that cellar and nobody would ever know. And it was getting there. I decided that I needed to do something about it. So every time I went to school, I passed a cafe, and this cafe was very popular with German soldiers. They came. It was off duty. They drank. They got drunk. And it was very close also to a Legale Duneaux in Belgium, which is a a train station which were, of course, the favorite targets for, for the Allied bombing. They wanted to stop them from convoys and arms shipping and all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had an awful lot of bombing going on in, in that particular neighborhood where we were hidden was just up the street. And this cafe was between the station and where we were hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I said, I saw them being drunk, not knowing who they were, what they were, staggering out. And I said that that would be possibly a good place. And they certainly had money on them too. If I needed to steal anything, this certainly would be a good place to do it. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So there came a time where I decided to just stay out one night and see what would happen. And the best way I can describe it today, you would call it a parking lot in the back of, of, of cafe. But it wasn't a parking lot. It was just a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to come out and throw up, or roll on the ground, or sing their songs. But they couldn't even stand up. I thought they will be easy targets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you might say I stalked them for about two or three days, and I just didn't know how to do it. I was just too small to handle it myself. And as I said previously, we had an arsenal of guns that I had picked up during the bombings and so on. And I thought, well, if something-- I can at least hold them up. I know how to hold a gun. I've seen 'em use it. And I've played with them many, many nights. What else did I have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw the Underground person who always carried a gun and always put it on the-- on the table, showed me how to use it. So I have quite a few Lugers, which was the German favorite gun. And I went out one night. And at first, this drunken soldier came out, and I thought I could hold him up and ask him for his money, which I did. But he didn't even understand what I was saying much less understood that this little boy over there wanted the money. And he just laughed and he just pushed me, and I shot him. And when he fell, I don't know whether he was dead or not. All I know is I went straight for his pockets, searched his pockets and I found money in it and ran and went home and showed it to my mother. She asked me, but she didn't want to know. I said, I just found it. And so that kept us for a week. And we ran out again, and it became easier, and easier, and easier. So I did that on a weekly basis almost. But what happened is that so much was happening in that cafe and around that area that it-- that it became-- I mean, they guarded it much more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a lot more surveillance, Gestapo, going back and forth with the cars, and so on that it became impossible to even go there and not be discovered, even in the back. When you first started to do this and were successful, each time was it a German soldier by himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Sometimes two.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes three. It just didn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were still vulnerable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very. They didn't know what was going on. They were-- they were totally out-- I mean, they were off duty, and this is what they did. They went to the cafe and drank and sang their songs. And by that time, I have come-- I had come to hate them anyway. I didn't know the difference between a German soldier, an SS, or a Gestapo. Their uniform was the uniform, and I had to survive. And I needed to survive. And I did what I had to do then. But when that became almost impossible, then it was-- money actually almost didn't buy food, because it was rationed anyway. So the money was good enough to, let's say, pay the rent. And I will tell you how much that means to me-- the word pay the rent-- a little bit later. It didn't buy food. The rations were rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it became necessary to do something, at least about eating, which we weren't doing anymore. And money, as I said, wasn't helping. So on the way to school again, there were stores, grocery stores. And they were attended by, of course, Gentile, Belgian Gentiles, who, I remember one particular one just before all this had happened, was not exactly in love with Jews. That, that much I do remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, well, if I can do it with German soldiers I can certainly hold up a store. That should be easy enough. It's only for food. I'm not looking for money. And that's what I started doing. But knowing that it was in my neighborhood and on the way from there to school, I don't think that it was-- you know, I didn't think that would be smart to continue to do that and then have to pass that store during the day to go to school. So I started venturing out, out of the neighborhood and doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what can I say? I, I did what I had to do to survive for money. I did what I had to do to survive for food. And that got us through, right through liberation. So this continued right up until 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1944 is when Belgium was liberated. September 1944, if my history is correct. And yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were about nine years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: About. Going on 10 or 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever come close to being caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I guess. Would I-- did I know it? No. I ran. I escaped. I mean, after-- the good thing about the business of the cafe is that shots and shooting on the streets were very common situations. By the Underground, there was a lot of, for lack of a better word, call it assassination. But I mean, the Underground were killing Germans on the street in the night. Germans were still discovering people that they were taking to either camps if they were Jews or, or Belgians that escaped camp. So shootings was not something that was something out of the ordinary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, they did-- they did not exactly come immediately. But certainly, I mean, between being there and doing what I had to do and going home, I imagine I escaped it a few times. So this provided you with the food and money to survive--

speaker: interviewee
text: --along with your mother until 1944. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened then. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when 1944 came along, I must tell you that although there was some jubilation and-- well, no. Let's go back up a little bit. Something else did happen. I guess I'm trying to forget most of it. Before liberation, we had finally gotten a hold-- through what I was doing-- of a little radio. And we were able to listen to, I believe today, that it was the BBC. And there were some news of the world and what was happening and what was going on in the world, and the proximity of the Allied troops, and the hopes that we finally had that this would be all over soon hopefully. And we heard about the French invasion, that they were into Paris, which would have been very close to Belgium. And then-- I'll never forget that day-- came the Battle of the Bulge, where we knew they had entered Belgium. And we said, well, any time now. Any day now. And [SIGHS] then, we heard the reversals in Bastogne of the Battle of the Bulge, where the Germans pushed back the Allies. And I said, oh, my god. Not again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, is this ever going to end or is it going to end? Because the news we were getting was just not very good. And the Germans were strong again and comin' back. And we thought we'd have to go all through this again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very frankly, that probably was the first time in my life I ever thought of-- I've had it. I can't do this any longer. I mean, suicide would have been the best thing for me. I, I just couldn't handle it anymore. And I decided that I, I had-- the Underground was then very, very active, you know, because of the possibility of liberation. And I knew that there were lots of things that they were doing, particularly in the station, the train station over there, that bombing was going on all the time. They were trying to stop convoys. They were trying to stop the troops from leaving Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of them came over to also tell us the news. He did come over every once in awhile to tell us some news. We told him, of course, of the conditions of Barniche but what difference would it make? What could we do at that time about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me if I wanted to be part of the liberation. I mean, it's just like asking me-- it just meant the world to me. If I could do anything to shorten this, this agony, I, I would do it. And I was made of another two instances to bring a package to the Gestapo headquarters and a package to Legale Duneaux in Brussels, Belgium, and to run, which is what I did. And those were bombs, and it was sabotage. And I was told them-- I was told where to put them and where to leave them. And so I felt as part of it. And I felt like I had a use for my life again. And maybe, maybe I had some, some part in shortening this. But finally, it came. Liberation, September 1944, which I remember [SIGHS] dancing and singing in the streets by the Belgians. But I must tell you, I do remember that at that time I was so empty, so drained that yes, I saw the British come in. I saw the Americans roll in. But I don't remember, at least not myself-- my mother was too weak to even go out-- but I don't remember joy and I don't remember celebration, because there was so much more to come after that. So much more.

speaker: interviewer
text: A resistance group you spoke about earlier, was this the resistance group? Or did the resistance group come later?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The resistance group was throughout the occupation. I believe in the United States they call it the FI. They were Interior Forces is what they're called. I call it FFE, because that's what I remember. And I remember the armbands and liberation saying FFE, but it was a Federation of Interior Forces Underground, Belgian Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you became part of this group in 1944?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in September?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a little bit before September when all this was-- yes. Around September, August or September 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the leader of this group? To whom did you report?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only person, the only name that I remember, because they weren't using last names in those days, I always remember Andre. And it was easy to remember, because it was also my name that I took in my hiding days, was Andre Barniche. I mean Dennis. My name was Andre Barniche, but Andre was the leader who I've never met.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you finally learn that the war was finally over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, naturally, as I said, we were getting the news in, in-- on the radio. And then one day-- I won't forget that day. There was an awful lot of commotion on the streets. Germans were running every which way. Trucks were, were moving. There were troops on the trucks. Guns were thrown all over the streets. There was some shootings going on. I finally, for the first time, saw the Underground through that famous little window in the cellar. And I lifted myself up and saw that the Underground was outside, with the bands, white arm bands, I'll never forget that. And they were shooting the Germans. And I knew that something very big was happening and it had to happen. And of course, the news was telling us that they were advancing. And then came screaming, and joy, and flags were- were coming out of the balconies and the windows and all of that. Then I finally ventured out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw very near this famous Legale Duneaux Station, the tanks rolling in with the British soldiers and the American soldiers and all of that. We knew it was liberation time, but then what? Who was I? Where were my brothers and sisters? What were we going to do? How were we going to live? Where were we going to go? What money would we use? It wasn't over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then of course, we still had hopes that some of my family had survived. We, of course, had then-- by then, heard about concentration camps, and the gassing, and the burning, and all of that. So we were hopeful. In fact, for probably three years, for three years after liberation, there was a Jewish organization in Belgium that had an office that would receive the names of the-- [SIGHS] I don't know what to call Holocaust survivors-- but they were coming back to their homes. And they would post these names daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother, from the day of liberation to probably two to three years after that, every day went to that office and sat with other people that had survived, and sat there waiting for those names to show up. And possibly maybe, her sons and her daughters would have been on there, but that never happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were once-- when I said Buchenwald at the beginning-- we were once looked and, and, and found by an American soldier that said that he had a message for us. He had liberated one of the concentration camps, turned out to be Buchenwald, as I remember it. And said-- this is all to my mother, so I am trying to remember because it just doesn't make any sense anymore to me. And due to the fact that I said that, I think males went to Buchenwald. There were no females at Buchenwald. And that he had seen a brother and her sister with our name that died the day of liberation. And so I don't know if that's real. I don't know if that's true, but based on the fact that I'm told that there were no females in Buchenwald, I'm beginning to wonder if I-- if I know what's right or wrong. All I know is they've never come back. I've never gotten a death certificate, of course. In fact, I still have the Red Cross searching today with the, the discovery of new names with the Russian government now releasing a lot of those things. And hopefully, someday I'll be able to, to know where they died and how they died. But no. No one came back. And for three years, my mother did that. What we were going to do at that point-- my sister did come back, by the way, from the convents. And as many hidden children are not doing still till today, we never, never spoke about our experiences, mine at the Barniche. And I only know this today, and I'm, I'm still-- I'm amazed myself that we never spoke about it. And I never heard about her experiences in the convent, although I do remember her once saying that it was not always the same convent. They were taken in the night, because there were searches in convents also from one to another to another. And she didn't wind up in the same convent as she was when she was first taken there. But she came back. Now, at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she come back immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for the three years you searched for the names of your brothers and sisters on the lists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you live with your mother? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the same area, or the same neighborhood. We didn't want to leave that neighborhood just in case my brothers and sister would come back. Where would they look? Where would they search? We didn't want to leave the community that they would know where we were. We left names at those houses. We left names in-- in certainly, the Barniche's were still alive. And however terrible they may have been, I don't think they would have denied information to my brothers and sisters if they came back if they were able to tell them where we would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took on a place very near the area where we were hidden, in the same community. But I was only near 11 at that point, and my sister was about 15 and 16. And we had no family. We had nobody left.
